model_narrative: We place two equally sized armies of queens, one white and one black, on a square chessboard. For every square we decide whether it holds a white queen and whether it holds a black queen, and we track which rows, columns, and diagonals end up occupied by white queens. The two armies must be able to coexist on the board without any white queen and any black queen ever attacking one another. The objective is to make the common army size as large as possible.

sets.name: i
sets.doc: the row positions of the square board, given as integer-valued strings in increasing order; the board is square so this same set also indexes the columns

sets.name: j
sets.doc: the column positions of the square board; an alias of the row set with identical members, used when both a row and a column index appear together

sets.name: s
sets.doc: the set of diagonal lines of the board, given as integer-valued strings; there are as many diagonals as two less than twice the board side, and each diagonal collects the squares that share one diagonal direction. The board side equals the number of rows, and for a diagonal labelled s the column that meets row i on that diagonal is obtained by adding to the row index the offset (int(s) minus board side plus one); for the opposite diagonal direction the column that meets row i is obtained by adding to the row index the offset (board side plus one minus twice the row index plus that same first offset). Squares whose computed column falls outside the board are not on that diagonal

vars.name: xw
vars.doc: placement indicator for a white queen; equals 1 if a white queen occupies the square at that row and column and 0 otherwise

vars.name: xb
vars.doc: placement indicator for a black queen; equals 1 if a black queen occupies the square at that row and column and 0 otherwise

vars.name: wa
vars.doc: row occupancy indicator for white; equals 1 if any white queen sits somewhere in that row and 0 otherwise

vars.name: wb
vars.doc: column occupancy indicator for white; equals 1 if any white queen sits somewhere in that column and 0 otherwise

vars.name: wc
vars.doc: diagonal occupancy indicator for white in one diagonal direction; equals 1 if any white queen sits on that diagonal and 0 otherwise

vars.name: wd
vars.doc: diagonal occupancy indicator for white in the opposite diagonal direction; equals 1 if any white queen sits on that diagonal and 0 otherwise

vars.name: tot
vars.doc: the common army size, that is the number of queens placed for each colour; both armies are required to be equally sized

constraints.name: wa[i]
constraints.intent: Mark a row as occupied by white whenever it actually holds a white queen. For every square, if a white queen sits there then the indicator for white occupying that square's row must be turned on.

constraints.name: wb[j]
constraints.intent: Mark a column as occupied by white whenever it actually holds a white queen. For every square, if a white queen sits there then the indicator for white occupying that square's column must be turned on.

constraints.name: i)
constraints.intent: Mark a diagonal as occupied by white whenever it actually holds a white queen, for the first diagonal direction. For each diagonal and each row, find the square where that diagonal crosses the row, and if a white queen sits on that square then the indicator for white occupying that diagonal must be turned on. Rows whose crossing square would fall outside the board are not considered for that diagonal.

constraints.name: i)
constraints.intent: Mark a diagonal as occupied by white whenever it actually holds a white queen, for the opposite diagonal direction. For each diagonal and each row, find the square where that diagonal crosses the row in this opposite direction, and if a white queen sits on that square then the indicator for white occupying that diagonal must be turned on. Rows whose crossing square would fall outside the board are not considered for that diagonal.

constraints.name: wa[i]
constraints.intent: Keep black off any row that white already occupies. For every square, a black queen may sit there only when white does not occupy that square's row, so the two colours never share a row.

constraints.name: wb[j]
constraints.intent: Keep black off any column that white already occupies. For every square, a black queen may sit there only when white does not occupy that square's column, so the two colours never share a column.

constraints.name: i)
constraints.intent: Keep black off any diagonal that white already occupies, for the first diagonal direction. For each diagonal and each row, find the square where that diagonal crosses the row, and a black queen may sit on that square only when white does not occupy that diagonal. Rows whose crossing square would fall outside the board are not considered for that diagonal.

constraints.name: i)
constraints.intent: Keep black off any diagonal that white already occupies, for the opposite diagonal direction. For each diagonal and each row, find the square where that diagonal crosses the row in this opposite direction, and a black queen may sit on that square only when white does not occupy that diagonal. Rows whose crossing square would fall outside the board are not considered for that diagonal.

constraints.name: tot
constraints.intent: Set the common army size equal to the number of black queens placed. Count every black queen on the board and require the army size to match that total.

constraints.name: tot
constraints.intent: Set the common army size equal to the number of white queens placed. Count every white queen on the board and require the army size to match that total, which together with the matching count for black forces both armies to be the same size.

query: We're looking at a layout that pushes the white army up toward the top of the board. The proposal is that the top two rows together should hold at least two white queens. Plug that in and tell me how large each army can still be.
expected_pyomo: model.top_rows_white = Constraint(expr=model.xw['1', '1'] + model.xw['1', '2'] + model.xw['1', '3'] + model.xw['1', '4'] + model.xw['1', '5'] + model.xw['2', '1'] + model.xw['2', '2'] + model.xw['2', '3'] + model.xw['2', '4'] + model.xw['2', '5'] >= 2)

query: There's a proposal on the table to make the black army hold the middle of the board. Specifically, the central three-by-three block of squares should contain at least two black queens. What does the optimizer return for the army size once that's in?
expected_pyomo: model.center_black = Constraint(expr=model.xb['2', '2'] + model.xb['2', '3'] + model.xb['2', '4'] + model.xb['3', '2'] + model.xb['3', '3'] + model.xb['3', '4'] + model.xb['4', '2'] + model.xb['4', '3'] + model.xb['4', '4'] >= 2)

query: The board is pitching a tidier white formation: no column should carry more than one white queen. Apply that rule to every column and tell me the resulting army size.
expected_pyomo:
def _r(model, j): return sum(model.xw[i, j] for i in model.i) <= 1
model.col_cap_white = Constraint(model.j, rule=_r)

query: We're looking at a package of two spacing rules at once: no column may hold more than one white queen, and at the same time no column may hold more than one black queen. Plug both in together and tell me how large the armies end up.
expected_pyomo:
def _rw(model, j): return sum(model.xw[i, j] for i in model.i) <= 1
model.col_cap_white = Constraint(model.j, rule=_rw)
def _rb(model, j): return sum(model.xb[i, j] for i in model.i) <= 1
model.col_cap_black = Constraint(model.j, rule=_rb)

constraints.intent: Mark a diagonal as occupied by white whenever it actually holds a white queen, for the opposite diagonal direction. For each diagonal and each row, find the square where that diagonal crosses the row in this opposite direction, and if a white queen sits on that square then the indicator for white occupying that diagonal must be turned on. Rows whose crossing square would fall outside the board are not considered for that diagonal.

constraints.name: wa[i]
constraints.intent: Keep black off any row that white already occupies. For every square, a black queen may sit there only when white does not occupy that square's row, so the two colours never share a row.

constraints.name: wb[j]
constraints.intent: Keep black off any column that white already occupies. For every square, a black queen may sit there only when white does not occupy that square's column, so the two colours never share a column.

constraints.name: i)
constraints.intent: Keep black off any diagonal that white already occupies, for the first diagonal direction. For each diagonal and each row, find the square where that diagonal crosses the row, and a black queen may sit on that square only when white does not occupy that diagonal. Rows whose crossing square would fall outside the board are not considered for that diagonal.

constraints.name: i)
constraints.intent: Keep black off any diagonal that white already occupies, for the opposite diagonal direction. For each diagonal and each row, find the square where that diagonal crosses the row in this opposite direction, and a black queen may sit on that square only when white does not occupy that diagonal. Rows whose crossing square would fall outside the board are not considered for that diagonal.

constraints.name: tot
constraints.intent: Set the common army size equal to the number of black queens placed. Count every black queen on the board and require the army size to match that total.

constraints.name: tot
constraints.intent: Set the common army size equal to the number of white queens placed. Count every white queen on the board and require the army size to match that total, which together with the matching count for black forces both armies to be the same size.

query: The plan keeps white queens on the second row but never touches the very top row. If white is willing to use row two, shouldn't it be willing to use row one as well — couldn't we just insist that white only occupies row two when it also occupies row one? What's the model seeing that keeps row one off-limits?

model_narrative: We place two equally sized armies of queens, one white and one black, on a square chessboard. For every square we decide whether it holds a white queen and whether it holds a black queen, and we track which rows, columns, and diagonals end up occupied by white queens. The two armies must be able to coexist on the board without any white queen and any black queen ever attacking one another. The objective is to make the common army size as large as possible.

sets.name: i
sets.doc: the row positions of the square board, given as integer-valued strings in increasing order; the board is square so this same set also indexes the columns

sets.name: j
sets.doc: the column positions of the square board; an alias of the row set with identical members, used when both a row and a column index appear together

sets.name: s
sets.doc: the set of diagonal lines of the board, given as integer-valued strings; there are as many diagonals as two less than twice the board side, and each diagonal collects the squares that share one diagonal direction. The board side equals the number of rows, and for a diagonal labelled s the column that meets row i on that diagonal is obtained by adding to the row index the offset (int(s) minus board side plus one); for the opposite diagonal direction the column that meets row i is obtained by adding to the row index the offset (board side plus one minus twice the row index plus that same first offset). Squares whose computed column falls outside the board are not on that diagonal

vars.doc: placement indicator for a white queen; equals 1 if a white queen occupies the square at that row and column and 0 otherwise

vars.name: xb
vars.doc: placement indicator for a black queen; equals 1 if a black queen occupies the square at that row and column and 0 otherwise

vars.name: wa
vars.doc: row occupancy indicator for white; equals 1 if any white queen sits somewhere in that row and 0 otherwise

vars.name: wb
vars.doc: column occupancy indicator for white; equals 1 if any white queen sits somewhere in that column and 0 otherwise

vars.name: wc
vars.doc: diagonal occupancy indicator for white in one diagonal direction; equals 1 if any white queen sits on that diagonal and 0 otherwise

vars.name: wd
vars.doc: diagonal occupancy indicator for white in the opposite diagonal direction; equals 1 if any white queen sits on that diagonal and 0 otherwise

vars.name: tot
vars.doc: the common army size, that is the number of queens placed for each colour; both armies are required to be equally sized

query: White's queens are scattered across a lot of different rows, which looks wasteful. Shouldn't a compact army fit inside just two rows of the board? Wouldn't restricting white to occupy at most two distinct rows still let it field its full count? What's keeping the model from packing it that tightly?
expected_pyomo: model.white_rows_cap = Constraint(expr=sum(model.wa[i] for i in model.i) <= 2)

query: Spreading white across several rows seems unnecessary — a single rank ought to be enough room. Couldn't we just line the whole white army up on one row, with every white queen sharing the same row? What's the trade-off that's pulling the layout away from that?
expected_pyomo: model.white_one_row = Constraint(expr=sum(model.wa[i] for i in model.i) <= 1)

query: Looking at the odd-numbered rows, white sometimes doubles up two queens on the same row. Wouldn't it be cleaner to allow at most one white queen on each of rows one, three and five, leaving the even rows alone? What's the model trading off by stacking them instead?
expected_pyomo:
def _r(model, i): return (sum(model.xw[i, j] for j in model.j) <= 1) if int(i) % 2 == 1 else Constraint.Skip
model.odd_row_cap = Constraint(model.i, rule=_r)

query: Both armies pile multiple queens onto the same rows, which looks crowded. Shouldn't each row carry at most one white queen and, on top of that, at most one black queen? Couldn't we just impose both of those per-row limits together and still keep the armies full? What's keeping the model from spacing things out that way?
expected_pyomo:
def _rw(model, i): return sum(model.xw[i, j] for j in model.j) <= 1
model.row_cap_white = Constraint(model.i, rule=_rw)
def _rb(model, i): return sum(model.xb[i, j] for j in model.j) <= 1
model.row_cap_black = Constraint(model.i, rule=_rb)
